key_points: The poem philosophically reflects on the resilience of nature and the moral consequences of human destruction.
It portrays the tree as a symbol of life, strength, and continuity, resistant to superficial human harm.
Personification and vivid imagery emphasize the suffering, endurance, and intrinsic life force of the tree.
Themes include environmental ethics, human responsibility, resilience of life, and the interconnectedness of humans and nature.
The poem critiques human cruelty and exploitation, encouraging deeper reflection on sustainable coexistence with nature.
examples: The tree bleeding when cut represents both literal damage and moral consequences of human violence.
Roots holding life even after the trunk is cut symbolize the enduring strength of nature and its philosophical significance.
The deliberate process of killing a tree metaphorically highlights human intentional harm versus natural resilience.
exam_tips: Interpret the tree as a symbol of life, resilience, and moral truth about human actions.
Analyze how personification and imagery convey suffering and endurance.
Discuss the poem’s philosophical reflection on environmental ethics and human responsibility.
Be able to explain symbolic and metaphorical meanings of cutting the tree, roots, and the life force it retains.
Focus on the deeper moral and reflective tone beyond the literal description of killing a tree.
